conditions.name: Acute Coronary Syndrome
conditions.recommendation: Arrange immediate hospital transport. Aspirin 325 mg chewed if no allergy. ECG within 10 minutes if available. Document onset time.
conditions.during_transport: Continuous vitals (BP, HR, SpO2). Second aspirin if ongoing pain. Avoid nitrates if SBP < 90. If cardiac arrest, start CPR.
conditions.history_trigger: Typical anginal character (pressure/squeezing, exertional, radiating to jaw or left arm) escalates to RED even with normal ECG/Echo/troponin — this is unstable angina.

conditions.name: Hypertensive Crisis
conditions.recommendation: Arrange immediate hospital transport. Document BP. Do NOT lower BP rapidly outside hospital — controlled reduction prevents stroke from over-correction.
conditions.during_transport: Recheck BP every 15 min. Avoid sudden lowering. Patient seated upright. Watch for neurologic deficit (suggests stroke from over-correction or hypertensive encephalopathy).

conditions.name: Diabetic Ketoacidosis
conditions.recommendation: Arrange immediate hospital admission for IV fluids and insulin. Do NOT give insulin without hospital monitoring (risk of cerebral edema in children). Check fingerstick glucose.
conditions.during_transport: IV access, normal saline if trained. Monitor consciousness. NPO. Oxygen if dyspneic. If patient deteriorates, check glucose — DKA can co-exist with concurrent illness.

conditions.name: Hypoglycemia
conditions.recommendation: If conscious: 15 g fast carbs (juice, sugar). Recheck glucose in 15 min. Repeat if still low. If unconscious or seizing: IM glucagon if available, otherwise sugar/honey on gums + arrange transport.
conditions.during_transport: If unconscious, recovery position, NPO. Monitor airway. IM glucagon if available. Notify hospital that patient is hypoglycemic (do not delay for IV access).

conditions.name: Pulmonary Embolism
conditions.recommendation: Arrange immediate hospital transport for CT angiography. Oxygen if SpO2 < 92%. Do NOT give heparin/anticoagulant outside hospital — confirm diagnosis first.
conditions.during_transport: Sit patient upright. Oxygen. Monitor for cardiac arrest (massive PE can cause sudden collapse). IV access if trained. Do not delay transport.

conditions.name: Acute Ischemic Stroke
conditions.recommendation: Arrange immediate hospital transport. Document time of symptom onset (thrombolytic window is 4.5 hours). Do NOT give aspirin until hemorrhagic stroke is ruled out by CT.
conditions.during_transport: Keep head elevated 30 degrees. NPO (nothing by mouth). Monitor BP — do not aggressively lower unless > 220/120. Glucose check (hypoglycemia mimics stroke).

conditions.name: Sepsis
conditions.recommendation: Arrange immediate hospital transport. IV access and fluid resuscitation if trained (30 mL/kg crystalloid). Oxygen. Document time of first symptom (1-hour bundle target).
conditions.during_transport: Continue fluids. Monitor BP, HR, SpO2, mental status. If hypotensive, lay flat with legs elevated. Notify hospital of suspected sepsis on arrival so antibiotics start within the first hour.

conditions.name: Pneumonia
conditions.recommendation: Chest X-ray, sputum culture, consider antibiotics based on severity

conditions.name: Severe Dehydration
conditions.recommendation: IV fluid replacement, electrolyte assessment, identify cause

conditions.name: Acute Myocardial Infarction
conditions.recommendation: Arrange emergency PCI-capable hospital transport. Aspirin 325 mg chewed. Oxygen if SpO2 < 90%. Call ahead so cath lab is ready.
conditions.during_transport: Cardiac monitoring. Second aspirin if pain persists. Nitrates only if SBP > 90. IV access. Defibrillator on standby.

conditions.name: Hypertensive Encephalopathy
conditions.recommendation: Immediate hospitalization, controlled BP reduction, ICU monitoring

conditions.name: Common Cold / Upper Respiratory Infection
conditions.recommendation: Supportive care, rest, fluids, OTC medications as needed

conditions.name: Influenza (Flu)
conditions.recommendation: Antiviral therapy if early, supportive care, monitor for complications

conditions.name: Anaphylaxis
conditions.recommendation: Epinephrine IM 0.3–0.5 mg into anterolateral thigh — give FIRST, before transport. Lay flat with legs elevated (do not stand patient up). Arrange immediate hospital transport. Repeat epinephrine every 5–15 min if no improvement.
conditions.during_transport: Patient supine. Oxygen. Monitor airway closely (delayed swelling possible). Be ready to repeat IM epinephrine. IV antihistamines and steroids are adjuncts only — epinephrine is the lifesaver.

conditions.name: Hyperglycemia / Hyperglycemic Crisis
conditions.recommendation: Blood glucose check, insulin adjustment, identify trigger

conditions.name: Major Trauma
conditions.guideline: ATLS 10th Edition + WHO Trauma Care Checklist
conditions.recommendation: Primary survey (Airway-Breathing-Circulation). Control external bleeding with direct pressure. Stabilize cervical spine if head/neck injury suspected. Arrange immediate hospital transport. Do NOT remove impaled objects.
conditions.during_transport: Maintain airway. Two large-bore IV lines if trained. Keep patient warm (hypothermia worsens bleeding). Monitor consciousness (GCS). Splint long-bone fractures. Document mechanism of injury and time.
conditions.history_trigger: High-velocity mechanism (road accident, fall from height) + any altered consciousness, chest/abdominal pain, or limb deformity → RED regardless of how the patient looks initially. Internal injuries can be silent for the first 30 minutes.

conditions.name: Acute Poisoning
conditions.recommendation: Identify the poison if possible (bring container or sample). Arrange immediate hospital transport. Do NOT induce vomiting. Do NOT give milk or water unless directed by Poison Control. For organophosphate (pesticide) exposure with drooling/small pupils/slow HR, atropine is the antidote — clinician judgement.
conditions.during_transport: Maintain airway (poisoning patients often vomit). Recovery position if unconscious. Bring the poison container/label with the patient. Monitor for seizures and respiratory failure. Note time of ingestion and amount.
conditions.history_trigger: Any history of ingestion + drooling, miosis (small pupils), bradycardia, or respiratory distress strongly suggests organophosphate — escalate immediately.

conditions.name: Snake Bite Envenomation
high_risk_factors: venomous species in region (cobra, krait, viper)
conditions.guideline: WHO Snake Bite Management 2017 + ICMR India Snakebite Treatment Protocol
conditions.recommendation: Arrange immediate hospital transport for anti-venom. Reassure the patient (panic raises HR and spreads venom). Immobilize the bitten limb in a splint, kept BELOW heart level. Remove rings/watches/tight clothing before swelling. Mark the leading edge of swelling on the skin with time noted.
conditions.during_transport: Keep limb immobilized and below heart level. Monitor for neurotoxic signs (drooping eyelids, drooling, weak breathing — indicates cobra/krait) and hemotoxic signs (gum bleeding, oozing — indicates viper). Photograph the snake ONLY if dead and safe.
conditions.history_trigger: Any reported snake bite in an endemic area is a potential emergency, even if patient looks well — neurotoxic envenomation can have a 30–60 minute lag before paralysis sets in.
conditions.folk_error_correction: DO NOT apply a tourniquet or tight tie — this concentrates venom and causes limb necrosis. DO NOT cut and suck the wound. DO NOT give alcohol or coffee. DO NOT wait to see if symptoms develop — transport now.
